source_metadata.title_raw: Scott C. Smith - A-19-008 - February 10, 2020 - Irvine

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Scott C. Smith 
San Clemente City Attorney 
Best Best and Krieger LLP 
18101 Von Karman Avenue, Suite 1000 
Irvine, CA 92612 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-008 
Dear Mr. Smith: 
February 10, 2020 
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of San Clemente City Mayor 
Dan Bane regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the Act) 1 and 
Section 1090. Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice and any advice we 
provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71) 
Under Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating 
to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County District Attorney's Office, 
which we have done. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )(3 ). ) We did not receive a written response from either 
entity. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 
1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other 
than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c )( 5).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. 
Does Mayor Bane have a disqualifying economic or financial interest under the 
Political Reform Act or Section 1090 regarding decisions relating to the City's litigation against the 
Foothill Eastern Transportation Corridor Agency (Foothill), by representing another plaintiff in the 
action and, if so, would the rule of necessity allow the City to enter an agreement to settle the 
litigation? 
2. 
May Mayor Bane continue to serve as a director for San Joaquin Hills Transportation 
Corridor Agency (Hills), which is a separate entity from Foothill and has no subject matter 
jurisdiction over Foothill matters, including pending litigation? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. 
Yes. Mayor Bane has a conflict of interest in the City's litigation and he must recuse 
himself from decisions regarding the City's litigation. Under the rule of necessity, the City may 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-19-008 
Page No. 2 
proceed with decisions regarding the litigation and may enter any potential contract related to the 
City's litigation against Foothill, including a settlement agreement. 
2. 
Yes. Mayor Bane is not precluded from serving as director of Hills. 
FACTS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTER 
A. 
Mayor Dan Bane. 
Dan Bane is a California attorney admitted to the bar in 2007 and joined Sheppard Mullin in 
2008. He represents a variety oflarge and small businesses, public agencies, property owners, 
developers, and other private parties involving compliance with the California Environmental 
Quality Act, the National Environmental Policy Act, the Endangered Species Act, and other State 
and local development and environmental regulations. 
Mr. Bane has been a profit-sharing, non-equity partner at his firm since 2016. His base 
compensation and bonuses are set by the firm's compensation committee, based on a holistic view 
of his performance. The committee considers origination of work and client receipts in setting 
compensation "tiers" and bonus, but they are not directly linked by formula to them. Rather, the 
committee sets compensation subjectively based on a partner's performance. Mr. Bane is also a 
participant in the firm's annual bonus pool for non-equity partners at a fixed percentage. This 
income pool is distributed to participants based again on a holistic view of individual performance. 
Mr. Bane was elected to the San Clemente City Council in November 2018. In 2019, he was 
elected to the Office of the Mayor by the City Council. Among many other things, Mayor Bane's 
duties on the City Council will include decisions relating to Foothill, such as which councilmember 
will serve as the delegate to the Foothill Board of Directors, whether to propose legislation 
curtailing Foothill's powers, and commenting on Foothill projects and their accompanying 
environmental documentation. Mayor Bane has decided to recuse himself from participating in all 
City Council decisions regarding the Litigation Matters. 
Mayor Bane was also appointed by the City Council to serve as (1) the City's representative 
on the Hills Board of Directors, and (2) the City's "alternate" on the Foothill Board of Directors. 
Mayor Bane took his seat on the Hills Board on February 14, 2019. Since his appointment as the 
Foothill alternate, however, Mr. Bane has never served in that capacity and has never participated in 
any meeting or otherwise participated in any Foothill Board decisions. Mr. Bane has resigned from 
the Foothill alternate position. 
B. 
Joint Powers Authorities. 
Several South Orange County Cities, including San Clemente, and the County of Orange are 
parties to two different joint powers agency (JPA) agreements formulated for the specific purpose 
of developing specific toll freeways in South County. These two JPA agreements formed two 
separate legal entities which have separate and distinct powers and liabilities, Foothill and Hills. 
Foothill is responsible for constructing a freeway linking SR-91 to 1-5 south of San 
Clemente. Foothill's decision to consider development of that freeway on a newly devised course 


FileNo. A-19-008 
Page No. 3 
through San Clemente is the subject of pending litigation by the City and The Reserve. Hills is 
separate from Foothill, with distinct powers and liabilities that are limited to the San Joaquin Hills 
Transportation Corridor. 
The Foothill and Hills JP As share staff and certain resources, but neither has decision-
making control over the other. Their respective boards hold joint meetings but matters relevant to 
Hills and Foothill respectively are noticed and acted upon through separate agendas. Their JPA 
Agreements include the possibility of joint projects which would require that a separate authority be 
created to undertake them. Thus, unless a Joint Transportation Corridor Agency is formed, the 
matters and decisions before one of these JP As are not relevant to the other. 
C. 
The Litigation Matters. 
Since the 1980s, the joint powers agreement and state legislation creating Foothill 
contemplated Foothill's construction of an extension of the SR-241 toll road from SR-91 in central 
Orange County to a connection with Interstate-5 south of San Clemente. The segment of that 
freeway from Oso Parkway in Mission Viejo to 1-5 south of San Clemente faced vehement 
opposition and prolonged litigation brought by a coalition of environmental groups and State 
agencies. In late 2016, Foothill abandoned those plans, formalized that abandonment in a settlement 
agreement with the environmental coalition and the State, and turned its attention to several 
alternative freeway routes, some of which would significantly disrupt the physical, environmental, 
and economic quality of life in San Clemente. 
The San Clemente City Council voted to challenge these Foothill decisions and authorized 
its attorneys, Best Best & Krieger LLP (BBK), to file litigation in 201 7 to set them aside and to stop 
any further study or pursuit of the San Clemente toll road alignments. In a separate matter, The 
Reserve, a 50l(c)(4), retained Mr. Bane and his law firm to file a similar lawsuit against Foothill in 
2017. The trial court consolidated these actions, which are still pending. 
1. 
Litigation Representation, Management, and Fees. 
The City and The Reserve (the plaintiffs) each separately direct its own law firm in their 
respective actions, including causes of action to allege, litigation strategies, and participation in and 
consideration of potential settlement. The plaintiffs entered into a common interest and 
confidentiality agreement (plaintiffs' agreement) under which their respective attorneys could share 
confidential attorney-client communications and work product. BBK and Sheppard Mullin have 
worked alongside each other in this manner, sharing information and coordinating efforts in filing 
motions and asserting arguments in the case. 
Plaintiffs work separately with their respective firms on litigation strategy and decisions. 
Although these cases may have common facts, law or witnesses that warrant consolidation and 
coordination of discovery and work-product, they are nevertheless separate actions that plaintiffs 
and their respective counsel have handled accordingly. For example, The Reserve sought discovery 
in the case, but the City decided to confine its efforts to an attack on the record. Foothill offered 
terms of settlement mid-2018 that the City summarily rejected, but The Reserve considered the 
settlement offer for several months. 


File No. A-19-008 
Page No. 4 
The Sheppard Mullin agreement with The Reserve provides for payment of hourly fees 
capped at $90,000, unless The Reserve prevails in the litigation. If The Reserve prevails, then it 
must pay the remaining fees incurred, with a 50% success premium. Any attorney's fees recovered 
would be applied to defray the amount owed by The Reserve. The Reserve is required to pay the 
fees regardless of whether the court awards them. 
The City has a separate contingent fee agreement with BBK. The City anticipates engaging 
separate counsel to evaluate any settlement proposal involving the potential payment of attorney's 
fees. 
2. 
Cooperative Agreement and Workplan. 
BBK and Sheppard Mullin, on behalf of their respective clients, have entered into a 
cooperative agreement (the cooperative agreement) regarding firms' plans and commitments to 
their continued representation of their respective clients. 
The cooperative agreement notes that the firms shall continue to independently represent 
their respective clients in their actions against Foothill. It sets forth a workplan which creates a 
division of labor for certain tasks in the consolidated litigation (workplan). The purpose of the 
workplan is to avoid unnecessary duplication of effort and to complement and peer review the other 
firm's work in the litigation. For example, an administrative record for the underlying writ of 
mandate causes of action must be fully prepared and certified, but there is currently a dispute 
between the plaintiffs and respondents about the content, scope, and use of a single omnibus 
administrative record for the litigation. The cooperative agreement states that the City will take the 
lead on administrative record issues. The cooperative agreement also recognizes several issues in 
which plaintiffs will need to handle matters separately. For example, Foothill filed a demurrer to the 
City's operative pleading, which the City researched and opposed on its own. 
ANALYSIS 
A. 
Conflict of Interest Under Section 1090 Regarding the City's Litigation. 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than noninterests or remote interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City ofTaft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to 
strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial 
Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
The prohibition applies even when the terms of the proposed contract are demonstrably fair and 
equitable, or are plainly to the public entity's advantage. (Id. at pp. 646-649.) Courts have 
recognized that Section 1090's prohibition must be broadly construed and strictly enforced. (Stigall 
v. City of Taft, supra, at pp. 569-571; Finnegan v. Schrader (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 572, 579-580; 


File No. A-19-008 
Page No. 5 
City Council v. McKinley (1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 213.) An important, prophylactic statute such 
as Section 1090 should be construed broadly to close loopholes, and should not be constricted and 
enfeebled. (Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1323, 1334.) 
1. 
Mayor Bane is subject to Section 1090 and the decision involves a contract. 
Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. Because the Mayor is a local officer for the City, he is subject 
to Section 1090. 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, we may examine the general 
principles of contract law. (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230,234 
(1995).) However, "specific rules applicable to Section 1090 require that we view the transactions 
in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 'contract."' (People v. Honig 
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 351 citing Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 571.) 
The litigation against Foothill may be resolved under a settlement agreement by one or both 
plaintiffs.2 "A settlement agreement is a contract, and the legal principles which apply to contracts 
generally apply to settlement contracts." (Weddington Productions, Inc. v. Flick (1998) 60 
Cal.App.4th 793, 810-811, citing Gorman v. Holte (1985) 164 Cal.App.3d 984, 988.) Courts have 
also held that characterized stipulated judgments or consent judgments as having the attributes of 
contracts. (See Gorman v. Holte, supra, 164 Cal.App.3d at p. 988.) Moreover, the Attorney General 
has opined that Section 1090 would prohibit a public official from participating in a settlement 
agreement in which the official is financially interested, and the body in which the official is a 
member could not enter the contract. (91 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 1 (2008); 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 142 
(2003).) Decisions regarding the litigation involve a contract. 
B. 
Mayor Bane would be making or participating in a contract. 
Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who execute a contract and casts a wide net to 
capture those officials who participate in any way in the making of the contract. (People v. Sobel 
(1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) Participation in the making of a contract is broadly defined as 
any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning and 
solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 
Cal.App.2d 222, 237.) An official is "expected to exercise absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance 
to the best interests of [his or her] governmental body or agency" and the objective of Section 1090 
"is to remove or limit the possibility of any personal influence, either directly or indirectly which 
may bear on an officer's or employee's decision." (Id.) 
Settlement negotiations, and deciding whether to enter a settlement agreement, require 
deliberation and discussion of litigation strategy. Thus, the Mayor Bane's participation in any City 
2 The facts provided set forth no specific decision at issue for the City regarding the litigation. Although we 
typically require a specific decision at issue to issue advice, a decision regarding a potential settlement agreement 
provides a sufficient example of a contract in the litigation process that enables us to analyze whether a disqualifying 
conflict of interest exists under Section 1090. Note that other contract decisions may arise in a civil lawsuit, such as 
retention of investigators, court reporters or expert witnesses. Also, based on the facts provided, The Reserve and the 
City have each entered a contract to retain attorneys for representation in the pending litigation. 


File No. A-19-008 
Page No. 6 
Council deliberation or discussion regarding an agreement to settle the litigation would constitute 
the making or participation in the making of a contract under Section 1090. 
C. 
Mayor Bane has a financial interest in a contract. 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might 
profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Prohibited financial interests may be 
indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the 
prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. 
Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
The term "financially interested" must be liberally interpreted under Section 1090. (See, 
e.g., People v. Deysher (1934) 2 Cal.2d 141, 146 ["(h)owever devious and winding the chain may 
be which connects the officer with the forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection 
made, the contract is void"].) The phrase "financially interested" includes anything that would tie a 
public official's fortunes to the existence of a public contract. ( Carson Redevelopment Agency v. 
Padilla, supra, at p. 1334.) "The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a 
public officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial 
gain as where he acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 
[ citations omitted].) 
As counsel of record for The Reserve in the consolidated action, Mayor Bane has a financial 
interest in litigation decisions by the City, which is both his client's co-plaintiff and an entity for 
which he serves as a public official. As discussed above, a settlement agreement is a contract. 
Mayor Bane's participation in discussions and deliberations regarding whether the City should enter 
a settlement agreement conflicts with his representation of both The Reserve and the City. In 
considering a settlement agreement for the City, the government would not have this public 
officer's absolute and undivided allegiance. At the very least, the circumstances create the 
appearance that the public officer's allegiance is divided between the City and The Reserve. For 
these reasons, Mayor Bane has a financial interest in a contract. 
D. 
Under the rule of necessity, the City may proceed with decisions without Mayor 
Bane's participation. 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (Dietrick Advice Letter, No. A-15-174; 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).) The rule of necessity ensures that essential government 
functions are performed even where a conflict of interest exists. (Ibid.) 
The rule of necessity has applied to permit a public officer to carry out the essential duties of 
the office despite a conflict of interest where only that officer may legally act. (65 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 310 (1982).) The rule applied to a city council where its members had a 
conflict of interest that otherwise would have prevented action by the board. (Federal Construction 
Co. v. Curd (1918) 179 Cal. 489, 493.) Where the rule of necessity applies to allow a multi-member 


File No. A-19-008 
Page No. 7 
body to act when it otherwise would have been precluded from doing so due to one or more 
members' conflicts of interest, the member or members with the conflict of interest must abstain 
from participation. (88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 111 (2005); 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102, 112 (1986); 
67 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 369, 378 (1984).) 
To determine whether the rule of necessity applies here, we examine whether litigation is an 
essential duty of the City Council and whether the City Council is the only government entity 
legally capable of settling or litigating these disputes. 
The City Council acts as the legislative body to set policy, approve the budget, pass local 
laws for the Municipal Code, and give direction on matters concerning the City. Given its broad 
authority over the City's policies and budget, addressing and resolving the litigation against Foothill 
is an essential duty of the City Council. If the City Council were precluded from participating in the 
litigation while Mayor Bane is in office, then no other government entity could act on the litigation 
without the City experiencing, among other things, loss of the relief sought, loss of due process, 
significant delay, or increased costs. For these reasons, the City Council is the only government 
entity legally capable of conducting the litigation for the City. Accordingly, we find that the rule of 
necessity applies to the potential litigation contracts at issue, including a settlement agreement. 
B. 
Mayor Bane's Service as Director of the Hills Board. 
Mayor Bane serves as director of the Hills board on behalf of the City, but Hills is not a 
party to the lawsuit. However, nothing in the Act or Section 1090 precludes an official from 
simultaneously serving on two governmental agencies. The Act only precludes an official from 
making, participating in making, or influencing a decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Similarly, Section 1090 only applies when an official has a financial interest in a contract. 
Based on the facts provided, you have not identified any decision before Hills that may implicate 
Mayor Bane's economic interests, including his firm, or any decision involving a contract before 
Hills in which Mayor Bane has a finical interest. Accordingly, we can only caution that Mayor 
Bane may be disqualified under the Act, and Section I 090 may apply, if a specific decision before 
Hills implicates any of his economic interests including his firm.3 
Based on the facts presented, we conclude that Section 1090 applies to any potential 
contract related to the City's litigation against Foothill. However, under the rule of necessity, the 
City Council may enter these potential contracts, but Mayor Bane must abstain from making or 
participating in the making of these contracts. 4 Mayor Bane is not precluded from serving as 
director of Hills. 
3 We also caution that laws outside the Act and Section 1090 may apply when serving on two governmental 
agencies, including provisions against incompatible activities and incompatible offices. However, the Commission does 
not have authority to advise on bodies of law outside of the Act and Section 1090. 
4 In light of the conclusion that Mayor Bane must recuse himself from decisions relating to the City's litigation 
under Section I 090, it is unnecessarily to analyze disqualification under the Act. However, we note that the Act sets 
forth specific procedures for a public official's proper recusal where the official is disqualified from participating in a 
governmental decision. For a city councilmember (or any other Section 87200 filers), the Act requires the official to 
publicly identify his or her interest and leave the room for the discussion of the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 
18707.) 


File No. A-19-008 
Page No. 8 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JMF: aja 
/ 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbriqge 
Ge~eFal Counsel

sections.question:
1. 
Does Mayor Bane have a disqualifying economic or financial interest under the 
Political Reform Act or Section 1090 regarding decisions relating to the City's litigation against the 
Foothill Eastern Transportation Corridor Agency (Foothill), by representing another plaintiff in the 
action and, if so, would the rule of necessity allow the City to enter an agreement to settle the 
litigation? 
2. 
May Mayor Bane continue to serve as a director for San Joaquin Hills Transportation 
Corridor Agency (Hills), which is a separate entity from Foothill and has no subject matter 
jurisdiction over Foothill matters, including pending litigation?
sections.conclusion:
1. 
Yes. Mayor Bane has a conflict of interest in the City's litigation and he must recuse 
himself from decisions regarding the City's litigation. Under the rule of necessity, the City mayproceed with decisions regarding the litigation and may enter any potential contract related to the 
City's litigation against Foothill, including a settlement agreement. 
2. 
Yes. Mayor Bane is not precluded from serving as director of Hills. 
FACTS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTER 
A. 
Mayor Dan Bane. 
Dan Bane is a California attorney admitted to the bar in 2007 and joined Sheppard Mullin in 
2008. He represents a variety oflarge and small businesses, public agencies, property owners, 
developers, and other private parties involving compliance with the California Environmental

and local development and environmental regulations. 
Mr. Bane has been a profit-sharing, non-equity partner at his firm since 2016. His base 
compensation and bonuses are set by the firm's compensation committee, based on a holistic view 
of his performance. The committee considers origination of work and client receipts in setting 
compensation "tiers" and bonus, but they are not directly linked by formula to them. Rather, the 
committee sets compensation subjectively based on a partner's performance. Mr. Bane is also a 
participant in the firm's annual bonus pool for non-equity partners at a fixed percentage. This 
income pool is distributed to participants based again on a holistic view of individual performance. 
Mr. Bane was elected to the San Clemente City Council in November 2018. In 2019, he was 
elected to the Office of the Mayor by the City Council. Among many other things, Mayor Bane's

sections.analysis:
A. 
Conflict of Interest Under Section 1090 Regarding the City's Litigation. 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than noninterests or remote interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City ofTaft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to 
strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial 
Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
The prohibition applies even when the terms of the proposed contract are demonstrably fair and 
equitable, or are plainly to the public entity's advantage. (Id. at pp. 646-649.) Courts have 
recognized that Section 1090's prohibition must be broadly construed and strictly enforced. (Stigall 
v. City of Taft, supra, at pp. 569-571; Finnegan v. Schrader (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 572, 579-580;  
City Council v. McKinley (1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 213.) An important, prophylactic statute such 
as Section 1090 should be construed broadly to close loopholes, and should not be constricted and 
enfeebled. (Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1323, 1334.) 
1. 
Mayor Bane is subject to Section 1090 and the decision involves a contract. 
Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. Because the Mayor is a local officer for the City, he is subject 
to Section 1090. 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, we may examine the general 
principles of contract law. (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230,234 
(1995).) However, "specific rules applicable to Section 1090 require that we view the transactions 
in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 'contract."' (People v. Honig 
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 351 citing Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 571.) 
The litigation against Foothill may be resolved under a settlement agreement by one or both 
plaintiffs.2 "A settlement agreement is a contract, and the legal principles which apply to contracts 
generally apply to settlement contracts." (Weddington Productions, Inc. v. Flick (1998) 60 
Cal.App.4th 793, 810-811, citing Gorman v. Holte (1985) 164 Cal.App.3d 984, 988.) Courts have 
also held that characterized stipulated judgments or consent judgments as having the attributes of 
contracts. (See Gorman v. Holte, supra, 164 Cal.App.3d at p. 988.) Moreover, the Attorney General 
has opined that Section 1090 would prohibit a public official from participating in a settlement 
agreement in which the official is financially interested, and the body in which the official is a 
member could not enter the contract. (91 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 1 (2008); 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 142 
(2003).) Decisions regarding the litigation involve a contract. 
B. 
Mayor Bane would be making or participating in a contract. 
Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who execute a contract and casts a wide net to 
capture those officials who participate in any way in the making of the contract. (People v. Sobel 
(1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) Participation in the making of a contract is broadly defined as 
any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning and 
solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 
Cal.App.2d 222, 237.) An official is "expected to exercise absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance 
to the best interests of [his or her] governmental body or agency" and the objective of Section 1090 
"is to remove or limit the possibility of any personal influence, either directly or indirectly which 
may bear on an officer's or employee's decision." (Id.) 
Settlement negotiations, and deciding whether to enter a settlement agreement, require 
deliberation and discussion of litigation strategy. Thus, the Mayor Bane's participation in any City 
2 The facts provided set forth no specific decision at issue for the City regarding the litigation. Although we 
typically require a specific decision at issue to issue advice, a decision regarding a potential settlement agreement 
provides a sufficient example of a contract in the litigation process that enables us to analyze whether a disqualifying 
conflict of interest exists under Section 1090. Note that other contract decisions may arise in a civil lawsuit, such as 
retention of investigators, court reporters or expert witnesses. Also, based on the facts provided, The Reserve and the 
City have each entered a contract to retain attorneys for representation in the pending litigation.  
Council deliberation or discussion regarding an agreement to settle the litigation would constitute 
the making or participation in the making of a contract under Section 1090. 
C. 
Mayor Bane has a financial interest in a contract. 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might 
profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Prohibited financial interests may be 
indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the 
prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. 
Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
The term "financially interested" must be liberally interpreted under Section 1090. (See, 
e.g., People v. Deysher (1934) 2 Cal.2d 141, 146 ["(h)owever devious and winding the chain may 
be which connects the officer with the forbidden contract, if it can be followed and the connection 
made, the contract is void"].) The phrase "financially interested" includes anything that would tie a 
public official's fortunes to the existence of a public contract. ( Carson Redevelopment Agency v. 
Padilla, supra, at p. 1334.) "The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a 
public officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial 
gain as where he acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 
[ citations omitted].) 
As counsel of record for The Reserve in the consolidated action, Mayor Bane has a financial 
interest in litigation decisions by the City, which is both his client's co-plaintiff and an entity for 
which he serves as a public official. As discussed above, a settlement agreement is a contract. 
Mayor Bane's participation in discussions and deliberations regarding whether the City should enter 
a settlement agreement conflicts with his representation of both The Reserve and the City. In 
considering a settlement agreement for the City, the government would not have this public 
officer's absolute and undivided allegiance. At the very least, the circumstances create the 
appearance that the public officer's allegiance is divided between the City and The Reserve. For 
these reasons, Mayor Bane has a financial interest in a contract. 
D. 
Under the rule of necessity, the City may proceed with decisions without Mayor 
Bane's participation. 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (Dietrick Advice Letter, No. A-15-174; 88 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).) The rule of necessity ensures that essential government 
functions are performed even where a conflict of interest exists. (Ibid.) 
The rule of necessity has applied to permit a public officer to carry out the essential duties of 
the office despite a conflict of interest where only that officer may legally act. (65 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 310 (1982).) The rule applied to a city council where its members had a 
conflict of interest that otherwise would have prevented action by the board. (Federal Construction 
Co. v. Curd (1918) 179 Cal. 489, 493.) Where the rule of necessity applies to allow a multi-member  
body to act when it otherwise would have been precluded from doing so due to one or more 
members' conflicts of interest, the member or members with the conflict of interest must abstain 
from participation. (88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 111 (2005); 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102, 112 (1986); 
67 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 369, 378 (1984).) 
To determine whether the rule of necessity applies here, we examine whether litigation is an 
essential duty of the City Council and whether the City Council is the only government entity 
legally capable of settling or litigating these disputes. 
The City Council acts as the legislative body to set policy, approve the budget, pass local 
laws for the Municipal Code, and give direction on matters concerning the City. Given its broad 
authority over the City's policies and budget, addressing and resolving the litigation against Foothill 
is an essential duty of the City Council. If the City Council were precluded from participating in the 
litigation while Mayor Bane is in office, then no other government entity could act on the litigation 
without the City experiencing, among other things, loss of the relief sought, loss of due process, 
significant delay, or increased costs. For these reasons, the City Council is the only government 
entity legally capable of conducting the litigation for the City. Accordingly, we find that the rule of 
necessity applies to the potential litigation contracts at issue, including a settlement agreement. 
B. 
Mayor Bane's Service as Director of the Hills Board. 
Mayor Bane serves as director of the Hills board on behalf of the City, but Hills is not a 
party to the lawsuit. However, nothing in the Act or Section 1090 precludes an official from 
simultaneously serving on two governmental agencies. The Act only precludes an official from 
making, participating in making, or influencing a decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Similarly, Section 1090 only applies when an official has a financial interest in a contract. 
Based on the facts provided, you have not identified any decision before Hills that may implicate 
Mayor Bane's economic interests, including his firm, or any decision involving a contract before 
Hills in which Mayor Bane has a finical interest. Accordingly, we can only caution that Mayor 
Bane may be disqualified under the Act, and Section I 090 may apply, if a specific decision before 
Hills implicates any of his economic interests including his firm.3 
Based on the facts presented, we conclude that Section 1090 applies to any potential 
contract related to the City's litigation against Foothill. However, under the rule of necessity, the 
City Council may enter these potential contracts, but Mayor Bane must abstain from making or 
participating in the making of these contracts. 4 Mayor Bane is not precluded from serving as 
director of Hills. 
3 We also caution that laws outside the Act and Section 1090 may apply when serving on two governmental 
agencies, including provisions against incompatible activities and incompatible offices. However, the Commission does 
not have authority to advise on bodies of law outside of the Act and Section 1090. 
4 In light of the conclusion that Mayor Bane must recuse himself from decisions relating to the City's litigation 
under Section I 090, it is unnecessarily to analyze disqualification under the Act. However, we note that the Act sets 
forth specific procedures for a public official's proper recusal where the official is disqualified from participating in a 
governmental decision. For a city councilmember (or any other Section 87200 filers), the Act requires the official to 
publicly identify his or her interest and leave the room for the discussion of the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 
18707.)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Does Mayor Bane have a disqualifying economic or financial interest under the 
Political Reform Act or Section 1090 regarding decisions relating to the City's litigation against the 
Foothill Eastern Transportation Corridor Agency (Foothill), by representing another plaintiff in the 
action and, if so, would the rule of necessity allow the City to enter an agreement to settle the 
litigation? 
2. 
May Mayor Bane continue to serve as a director for San Joaquin Hills Transportation 
Corridor Agency (Hills), which is a separate entity from Foothill and has no subject matter 
jurisdiction over Foothill matters, including pending litigation?

CONCLUSION: 1. 
Yes. Mayor Bane has a conflict of interest in the City's litigation and he must recuse 
himself from decisions regarding the City's litigation. Under the rule of necessity, the City mayproceed with decisions regarding the litigation and may enter any potential contract related to the 
City's litigation against Foothill, including a settlement agreement. 
2. 
Yes. Mayor Bane is not precluded from serving as director of Hills. 
FACTS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTER 
A. 
Mayor Dan Bane. 
Dan Bane is a California attorney admitted to the bar in 2007 and joined Sheppard Mullin in 
2008. He represents a variety oflarge and small businesses, public agencies, property owners, 
developers, and other private parties involving compliance with the California Environmental 
Quality Act, the National Environmental Policy Act, the Endangered Species Act, and other State 
and local development and environmental regulations. 
Mr. Bane has been a profit-sharing, non-equity partner at his firm since 2016. His base 
compensation and bonuses are set by the firm's compensation committee, based on a holistic view 
of his performance. The committee considers origination of work and client receipts in setting 
compensation "tiers" and bonus, but they are not directly linked by formula to them. Rather, the 
committee sets compensation subjectively based on a partner's performance. Mr. Bane is also a 
participant in the firm's annual bonus pool for non-equity partners at a fixed percentage. This 
income pool is distributed to participants based again on a holistic view of individual performance. 
Mr. Bane was elected to the San Clemente City Council in November 2018. In 2019, he was 
elected to the Office of the Mayor by the City Council. Among many other things, Mayor Bane's 
duties on the City Council will include decisions relating to Foothill, such as which councilmember 
will serve as the delegate to the Foothill Board of Directors, whether to propose legislation 
curtailing Foothill's powers, and commenting on Foothill projects and their accompanying 
environmental documentation. Mayor Bane has decided to recuse himself from participating in all 
City Council decisions regarding the Litigation Matters. 
Mayor Bane was also appointed by the City Council to serve as (1) the City's representative 
on the Hills Board of Directors, and (2) the City's "alternate" on the Foothill Board of Directors. 
Mayor Bane took his seat on the Hills Board on February 14, 2019. Since his appointment as the 
Foothill alternate, however, Mr. Bane has never served in that capacity and has never participated in 
any meeting or otherwise participated in any Foothill Board decisions. Mr. Bane has resigned from 
the Foothill alternate position. 
B. 
Joint Powers Authorities. 
Several South Orange County Cities, including San Clemente, and the County of Orange are 
parties to two different joint powers agency (JPA) agreements formulated for the specific purpose 
of developing specific toll freeways in South County. These two JPA agreements formed two 
separate legal entities which have separate and distinct powers and liabilities, Foothill and Hills. 
Foothill is responsible for constructing a freeway linking SR-91 to 1-5 south of San 
Clemente. Foothill's decision to consider development of that freeway on a newly devised course 


FileNo. A-19-008  
through San Clemente is the subject of pending litigation by the City and The Reserve. Hills is 
separate from Foothill, with distinct powers and liabilities that are limited to the San Joaquin Hills 
Transportation Corridor. 
The Foothill and Hills JP As share staff and certain resources, but neither has decision-
making control over the other. Their respective boards hold joint meetings but matters relevant to 
Hills and Foothill respectively are noticed and acted upon through separate agendas. Their JPA 
Agreements include the possibility of joint projects which would require that a separate authority be 
created to undertake them. Thus, unless a Joint Transportation Corridor Agency is formed, the 
matters and decisions before one of these JP As are not relevant to the other. 
C. 
The Litigation Matters. 
Since the 1980s, the joint powers agreement and state legislation creating Foothill 
contemplated Foothill's construction of an extension of the SR-241 toll road from SR-91 in central 
Orange County to a connection with Interstate-5 south of San Clemente. The segment of that 
freeway from Oso Parkway in Mission Viejo to 1-5 south of San Clemente faced vehement 
opposition and prolonged litigation brought by a coalition of environmental groups and State 
agencies. In late 2016, Foothill abandoned those plans, formalized that abandonment in a settlement 
agreement with the environmental coalition and the State, and turned its attention to several 
alternative freeway routes, some of which would significantly disrupt the physical, environmental, 
and economic quality of life in San Clemente. 
The San Clemente City Council voted to challenge these Foothill decisions and authorized 
its attorneys, Best Best & Krieger LLP (BBK), to file litigation in 201 7 to set them aside and to stop 
any further study or pursuit of the San Clemente toll road alignments. In a separate matter, The 
Reserve, a 50l(c)(4), retained Mr. Bane and his law firm to file a similar lawsuit against Foothill in 
2017. The trial court consolidated these actions, which are still pending. 
1. 
Litigation Representation, Management, and Fees. 
The City and The Reserve (the plaintiffs) each separately direct its own law firm in their 
respective actions, including causes of action to allege, litigation strategies, and participation in and 
consideration of potential settlement. The plaintiffs entered into a common interest and 
confidentiality agreement (plaintiffs' agreement) under which their respective attorneys could share 
confidential attorney-client communications and work product. BBK and Sheppard Mullin have 
worked alongside each other in this manner, sharing information and coordinating efforts in filing 
motions and asserting arguments in the case. 
Plaintiffs work separately with their respective firms on litigation strategy and decisions. 
Although these cases may have common facts, law or witnesses that warrant consolidation and 
coordination of discovery and work-product, they are nevertheless separate actions that plaintiffs 
and their respective counsel have handled accordingly. For example, The Reserve sought discovery 
in the case, but the City decided to confine its efforts to an attack on the record. Foothill offered 
terms of settlement mid-2018 that the City summarily rejected, but The Reserve considered the 
settlement offer for several months.  
The Sheppard Mullin agreement with The Reserve provides for payment of hourly fees 
capped at $90,000, unless The Reserve prevails in the litigation. If The Reserve prevails, then it 
must pay the remaining fees incurred, with a 50% success premium. Any attorney's fees recovered 
would be applied to defray the amount owed by The Reserve. The Reserve is required to pay the 
fees regardless of whether the court awards them. 
The City has a separate contingent fee agreement with BBK. The City anticipates engaging 
separate counsel to evaluate any settlement proposal involving the potential payment of attorney's 
fees. 
2. 
Cooperative Agreement and Workplan. 
BBK and Sheppard Mullin, on behalf of their respective clients, have entered into a 
cooperative agreement (the cooperative agreement) regarding firms' plans and commitments to 
their continued representation of their respective clients. 
The cooperative agreement notes that the firms shall continue to independently represent 
their respective clients in their actions against Foothill. It sets forth a workplan which creates a 
division of labor for certain tasks in the consolidated litigation (workplan). The purpose of the 
workplan is to avoid unnecessary duplication of effort and to complement and peer review the other 
firm's work in the litigation. For example, an administrative record for the underlying writ of 
mandate causes of action must be fully prepared and certified, but there is currently a dispute 
between the plaintiffs and respondents about the content, scope, and use of a single omnibus 
administrative record for the litigation. The cooperative agreement states that the City will take the 
lead on administrative record issues. The cooperative agreement also recognizes several issues in 
which plaintiffs will need to handle matters separately. For example, Foothill filed a demurrer to the 
City's operative pleading, which the City researched and opposed on its own.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Scott C. Smith San Clemente City Attorney Best Best and Krieger LLP 18101 Von Karman Avenue, Suite 1000 Irvine, CA 92612 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-008 Dear Mr. Smith: February 10, 2020 This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of San Clemente City Mayor Dan Bane regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the Act) 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71) Under Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )(3 ). ) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c )( 5).) QUESTIONS 1. Does Mayor Bane have a disqualifying economic or financial interest under the Political Reform Act or Section 1090 regarding decisions relating to the City's litigation against the Foothill Eastern Transportation Corridor Agency (Foothill), by representing another plaintiff in the action and, if so, would the rule of necessity allow the City to enter an agreement to settle the litigation? 2. May Mayor Bane continue to serve as a director for San Joaquin Hills Transportation Corridor Agency (Hills), which is a separate entity from Foothill and has no subject matter jurisdiction over Foothill matters, including pending litigation? CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. Mayor Bane has a conflict of interest in the City's litigation and he must recuse himself from decisions regarding the City's litigation. Under the rule of necessity, the City may 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-008 Page No. 2 proceed with decisions regarding the litigation and may enter any potential contract related to the City's litigation against Foothill, including a settlement agreement. 2. Yes. Mayor Bane is not precluded from serving as director of Hills. FACTS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTER A. Mayor Dan Bane.
